source: Generating Alpha — Ep.56: career arc, semis, NVDA/Tesla, AI infra cycles
summary: We are not in a valuation bubble in tech; multiples have compressed and tech now trades at a discount to staples
summary_extended: Baker argues that the widespread narrative of a tech valuation bubble is factually incorrect and reflects a lack of attention to actual market data. He states that tech multiples are at the same level they were five to six years ago, having compressed meaningfully since early 2025, and that tech now trades at a discount to consumer staples — a historically rare and notable occurrence. His reasoning is that bubble-callers are conflating high absolute prices or strong performance with stretched valuations, when in reality the earnings growth of tech companies has kept pace with or exceeded price appreciation. The investment implication is that tech broadly is not overvalued and may in fact represent relative value versus defensive sectors, suggesting investors should not underweight tech on valuation grounds alone.
detail: Baker explicitly states tech is at the same multiple as 5-6 years ago, multiples have compressed since early 2025, and tech is now at a discount to staples — a rare occurrence. Anyone claiming a valuation bubble is 'not paying attention.'
confidence_evidence: Stated emphatically and directly, framed as obvious fact against consensus bubble-callers
quote_fragment: tech's at the same multiple it was at five or six years ago
